147 Avenue A_ _Peace Eye on Avenue A

MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS


  1. "One-four-seven Avenue A, where the borough breathes / Built in nineteen-hundred, counterculture seeds" → Anchored in the Oasis Map confirmation: "Was built in 1900." Unknown Author. Oasis Map, p. 1.


    [13] "Strictly Kosher signs left hanging on the wall" → Drawn directly from the Rock and Roll Explorer account: "It read 'Strictly Kosher' in both English and Hebrew. He left it that way." Katz & Kott. (2018) Rock and Roll Explorer Guide to New York, p. 142.


    [10] "Beatnik to hippie, one storefront held it all" → Inspired by the biographical description: "A poet providing a bridge between Beatnik and Hippie generation." Patterson. (2012) Jews a Peoples History of the lower East side Volume 3, p. 226.


    [13] "Peace Eye on Avenue A, the block never sleeps" → Pulled from the Peace Eye Bookstore entry: "The Peace Eye became a popular destination and meeting place for the burgeoning East Village music and literary scene." Katz & Kott. (2018) Rock and Roll Explorer Guide to New York,

    p. 142.


    [4] "Five stories of brick where the underground creeps" → Referenced from the 1897

    Bromleys Atlas: "a 5 story brick building, a brick building." Bromley. 1897 Bromleys Atlas, p. 17.


    [12] "From Avenue A where the dissidents stood" → Drawn from the timeline noting: "Peace-Eye will reopen at 147 Ave. A." Patterson. (2007) Resistance, p. 243.


    1. "East Side to East Village — raised this neighborhood" → Inspired by the historical naming discussion: "Forget about the 'East Village.' This phrase was not turned until the 1960s." Tosches. (2005) King of the Jews, p. 123.


      [5] "Eighteen-fifty-three, a dwelling lines the street" → Drawn from the 1853 Perris Maps: "a Brick or stone dwelling first class (slate or metal roof & coped) with stores under." Perris. 1853 Perris Maps, p. 48.


  2. "By eighty-five, two brick buildings on the beat" → Pulled from the 1885 Robinson Atlas: "there is reference to 2 structures; a Brick Building, a Brick Building." Robinson. 1885 Robinson Atlas, p. 11.

  3. "Ninety-seven, five stories reaching for the sky" → References the 1897 Bromleys Atlas: "a

5 story brick building." Bromley. 1897 Bromleys Atlas, p. 17.


  1. "Nineteen-sixteen, that storefront never said goodbye" → Pulled from the 1916 Atlas entry: "a 5 story brick building with store with 5 story brick rear." G W Bromley & Co. 1916 Atlas of the Borough of Manhattan, p. 40.


    1. "Eighteen-sixty-seven — not a structure stood" → Directly from the Dripps Plan: "there is

      reference to 0 structures." Dripps. 1867 Dripps Plan, p. 7.


    2. "Then nineteen-hundred came and raised this neighborhood" → Again anchored in the

    Oasis Map: "Was built in 1900." Unknown Author. Oasis Map, p. 1.


  2. "A poet from Missouri, underground in every zone" → Drawn from the biographical note: "Born August 17, 1939. Kansas City, Missouri." Patterson. (2012) Jews a Peoples History of the lower East side Volume 3, p. 226.


[16] "Comix on the walls — Crumb, Spiegelman, set the tone" → Directly inspired by: "That year it featured an exhibition of the new school of underground comix artists, including R. Crumb, Spain Rodriguez, Kim Deitch, Art Spiegelman, and others." Katz & Kott. (2018) Rock and Roll Explorer Guide to New York, p. 143.


[7] "Nineteen-thirty land book, five stories with the store" → Pulled from the 1930 Land Book:

"a 5 story Building with Store." Bromley. 1930 Land Book of the Borough of Manhattan, p. 39.


[6] "Fifty-five Manhattan, brick and concrete to the core" → Referenced from the 1955 Manhattan Land Book: "5 Story Brick or Stone or Concrete building with Store." Bromley. 1955 Manhattan Land Book, p. 29.


[2] "One-four-seven Avenue A — Manhattan's layered lore" → A closing synthesis anchored

again in the Oasis Map's foundational record of the building. Unknown Author. Oasis Map, p. 1.
